stem: Let \( f(x) = [[a]]x^{2} + ([[b]])x + ([[c]]) \) on \( [[[p]], [[q]]] \). The Mean Value Theorem guarantees a value \( c \) in \( ([[p]], [[q]]) \) with \( f'(c) = \dfrac{f([[q]]) - f([[p]])}{[[q]] - [[p]]} \). Find \( c \).
solution: Since \( f \) is quadratic, \( f'(x)=2[[a]]x+([[b]]) \) is linear, so the guaranteed point is always the midpoint of the interval: \( c = \dfrac{[[p]]+[[q]]}{2} = [[answer]] \), regardless of the coefficients \( a,b,c \).
distractor_rationales: This gives the left endpoint of the interval, not the guaranteed interior point.
This gives the right endpoint of the interval, not the guaranteed interior point.
This reports the interval's width instead of its midpoint.
This is off by one from the correct midpoint.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: A particle's position at time \( t \) seconds is \( s(t) = [[a]]t^{2} + ([[b]])t \) meters. By the Mean Value Theorem, there is some instant \( t=c \) in \( ([[t1]], [[t2]]) \) at which the particle's instantaneous velocity \( s'(c) \) equals its AVERAGE velocity over \( [[[t1]], [[t2]]] \). Find \( c \).
solution: Since \( s \) is quadratic, \( s'(t)=2[[a]]t+([[b]]) \) is linear, so the guaranteed instant is the midpoint of the time interval: \( c = \dfrac{[[t1]]+[[t2]]}{2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the start of the time interval, not the guaranteed interior instant.
This is the end of the time interval, not the guaranteed interior instant.
This reports the elapsed time instead of the midpoint instant.
This is off by one from the correct midpoint.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x) = \dfrac{1}{x} \) on \( [[[p]], [[q]]] \) (with \( 0 < [[p]] < [[q]] \)). The Mean Value Theorem guarantees a value \( c \) in \( ([[p]], [[q]]) \) with \( f'(c) = \dfrac{f([[q]]) - f([[p]])}{[[q]] - [[p]]} \). Find \( c \) (take the positive root).
solution: \( f'(x)=-\dfrac{1}{x^{2}} \), and the secant slope is \( \dfrac{1/[[q]] - 1/[[p]]}{[[q]]-[[p]]} = -\dfrac{1}{[[p]][[q]]} \). Setting \( -\dfrac{1}{c^{2}} = -\dfrac{1}{[[p]][[q]]} \) gives \( c^{2}=[[p]][[q]] \), so (taking the positive root in \( ([[p]],[[q]]) \)) \( c=\sqrt{[[p]][[q]]} = [[answer]] \), the geometric mean.
distractor_rationales: This is the arithmetic mean, which is the MVT point for a LINEAR or quadratic function, not \( 1/x \).
This is the product \( pq \) itself, not its square root.
This takes the square root of the sum instead of the product.
This halves the product instead of taking its square root.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x) = x^{3} \) on \( [[[p]], [[q]]] \). The Mean Value Theorem guarantees a value \( c \) in \( ([[p]], [[q]]) \) with \( f'(c) = \dfrac{f([[q]]) - f([[p]])}{[[q]] - [[p]]} \). Find \( c \) (take the positive root).
distractor_rationales: This is the midpoint formula, which is only the MVT point for a quadratic, not a cubic.
This is the geometric mean, which is the MVT point for \( 1/x \), not \( x^{3} \).
This averages the squares of the endpoints instead of including the cross term \( pq \).
This forgets to take the square root after dividing by 3.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x) = [[a]]x^{2} + ([[b]])x + ([[c]]) \). On the interval \( ([[p]], q) \), the Mean Value Theorem's guaranteed point is \( c = [[cval]] \), i.e. \( f'([[cval]]) = \dfrac{f(q) - f([[p]])}{q - [[p]]} \). Find \( q \) (given \( q > [[p]] \)).
solution: Since \( f \) is quadratic, the guaranteed point is always the midpoint: \( [[cval]] = \dfrac{[[p]]+q}{2} \). Solving for \( q \) gives \( q = 2([[cval]]) - ([[p]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This computes the interval's width instead of solving the midpoint equation for \( q \).
This adds instead of solving \( c=(p+q)/2 \) for \( q \).
This drops the minus sign when isolating \( q \) from the midpoint equation.
This halves \( c \) before doubling, rather than doubling \( c \) first.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
